title: Indirect taxes & subsidies
summary:
## Indirect taxes

An **indirect tax** is a tax on *spending/expenditure*, collected by producers and passed on (wholly or partly) to consumers. Governments use them to raise revenue and to **correct market failure** (e.g. taxing demerit goods and negative externalities).

Two types:
- **Specific (unit) tax** — a fixed amount per unit (e.g. \$2 per litre). Shifts supply **up by a constant vertical distance** (parallel shift).
- **Ad valorem tax** — a percentage of the price (e.g. VAT at 15%). Shifts supply up by a **growing** amount as price rises (curves diverge).

Effect: supply shifts **left/up**, equilibrium **price rises** and **quantity falls**.

## Incidence of a tax

The **incidence (burden)** is how the tax is split between consumers and producers, and it depends on **relative elasticities**:
- **Inelastic demand** (or elastic supply) → consumers bear **most** of the tax (price rises a lot, quantity barely falls).
- **Elastic demand** (or inelastic supply) → producers bear **most** of the tax.
> This is why governments tax goods with **inelastic demand** (tobacco, fuel) — big revenue, small fall in quantity.

On the diagram: the tax is the vertical gap between the two supply curves; consumer incidence is the part above the old price, producer incidence the part below. The rectangle **(tax × new quantity)** is government revenue; the triangle beyond is **deadweight loss**.

## Subsidies

A **subsidy** is a payment from government to producers (or consumers) to *lower price and raise output* — used to encourage merit goods, positive externalities, or support key industries/incomes.

Effect: supply shifts **right/down**, equilibrium **price falls** and **quantity rises**.
- The **benefit is shared** between consumers (lower price) and producers (higher price received), again according to **relative elasticities**.
- **Cost** to government = subsidy per unit × new quantity — an **opportunity cost** for the budget.

## Evaluation

- Taxes/subsidies can internalise externalities but the **correct size is hard to set** (information problem) and demand may be **inelastic**, so quantity changes little.
- Taxes are **regressive** (hit the poor proportionately harder) and can push activity into black markets.
- Subsidies are **costly**, can encourage inefficiency/dependency, and may be captured by producers.

## Why it matters

Taxes and subsidies are the government's main *market-based* tools. They apply demand, supply, elasticity and surplus all at once, and they recur in externalities (A2 Unit 8) and fiscal policy (Unit 5).

keyTerms.definition: A tax on spending, collected by firms and passed on to consumers in higher prices.
A fixed tax per unit, causing a parallel upward shift of supply.
A percentage tax on price, causing supply to pivot up by a growing amount.
The division of the tax burden between consumers and producers, set by relative elasticities.
A government payment to lower price and raise output of a good.
The welfare loss from output moving away from the efficient equilibrium after a tax.

diagrams: **Specific tax:** supply shifts up by a constant vertical distance (S → S+tax); mark new higher price, lower quantity, and split the tax gap into consumer and producer incidence.
**Ad valorem tax:** supply pivots upward (widening gap); otherwise labelled like the specific-tax diagram.
**Tax revenue & deadweight loss:** shade the rectangle (tax × Q) as revenue and the triangle as deadweight loss.
**Subsidy:** supply shifts down/right; mark lower price, higher quantity, and the government cost rectangle.
tips: Distinguish **specific vs ad valorem** by the *shape* of the supply shift (parallel vs diverging).
Link **incidence to elasticity**: inelastic demand → consumer bears more. This is a very common exam point.
Explain why demerit goods are taxed (inelastic demand → high revenue, small quantity fall) — a ready evaluation line.
For subsidies, always state the **cost to government / opportunity cost** and who benefits most (by elasticity).
Evaluate the **information problem** — setting the ‘right’ tax/subsidy to fully correct an externality is difficult.

channels.why: Clear tax/subsidy diagrams with incidence analysis.
